message:
No one with a full time job in a first world country is being "robbed" as you suggest. You have the freedom to go apply for any job, anywhere. You have the freedom to demand any salary from any employer you wish (and if you are really worth that salary and can prove it then potential employers would be fools not to hire you). Also, and here's the most important part that first world socialists tend to overlook, you are free to risk your savings/time in the market or in business on your own. This year, many thousands of Americans young and old will quit their jobs and attempt to start their own businesses with their own money. Many if not the majority of them will fail, and that's fine. Some will succeed and do reasonably well but likely won't have much if any sort of net-positive global impact and will instead service smaller local/online communities (and end up making salaries/earnings that don't seem ridiculous to you). And a very small few will be successful beyond their wildest dreams and makes countless millions if not billions of dollars. Of these 3 groups of entrepreneurs, which do you think will have the largest net-positive economic impact on the world? If the possibility of increasing your earnings on an exponential scale is what drives many to risk everything and start their own business (and thus bring far more economic value to the marketplace than any individual low-mid level worker could) then why are we describing that as a moral problem? With great reward also comes great risk. Given that you claim to be an economics student then surely you aware of the fact that if we reward everyone equally then we completely negate any incentive for people to take risks (and thus innovate/create economic growth). 

Given that its incredibly obvious to anyone with two eyes that mere wealth inequality (regardless of poverty levels/standard of living) is a moral problem then I must be blind. The richest people in the world (Gates, Buffet, Bezos, Jobs to name a few) have provided far more economic value and time value to my life than any government has, let alone any mid-low level worker. I feel my life consistently improving year over year because of technological innovation and ease of access to the global marketplace. You know who really fleeces me and those I love every year? The US government and the US Federal Reserve.

It's not relevant, but quite obviously, yes. I've worked for-profit and non-profit. I've been overpaid and underpaid. I have an economics degree and work in finance. I'm telling you you're being fleeced by your bosses.

But part of the grotesqueness of this problem is that you need no more qualifications than having two eyes to understand enormous wealth inequality and how it hurts workers and the public in general. **Everyone** is fleeced by the ultrarich.

@complicated-area, wow, you think you're so smart with your fancy economics degree and your "experience" in finance. Newsflash: just because you've worked for-profit and non-profit doesn't mean you're some kind of expert on the subject. And by the way, what's with the condescending tone? You're not helping your case, buddy.

Oh great, another genius economist who thinks they can lecture me on the basics of economics. @wan-skin, sweetie, I've got a newsflash for you: just because you've got a degree and a job in finance doesn't mean you're a visionary. I've got a degree too, and I know a thing or two about the real world. And by the way, what's with the condescending tone? You're the one who's being condescending, not me. I'm just calling out your lack of original thought.

message:
I do, actually.

While it is true that the Federal Gov't could turn the entire country into a sheet of glass if it wanted to, that wouldn't be the goal should rebellion actually break out.  When a government is looking to quell insurrection, their ultimate goal is the reintegration of those rebel strongholds into the nation.  Nuking a rebellious state would be a _terrible_ idea - no one wants to be "king of the ashes" as the saying goes.

Most likely, any attempt to stop rebellion would look like our current wars in Iraq and Afghanistan.  The rebels there are quite successful defending themselves against the United States with just small arms.

Will they ever win?  Probably not, but the goal of rebellion isn't necessarily to _defeat_ the opposition - it is just to get them to leave you alone.

message: Nothing for renter's, immigrants (a bit of student visa Medicare exemption if your country has a treaty with the USA), single and childless people. I tick all those checkboxes. I already earn less than my American counterparts (because "we have to sponsor your visa and pay for attorneys and blah"), pay more taxes than any of them, and have a $40k loan on my head at 13% interest. (Cannot borrow in the USA before six years of residence.) To top all that, USCIS has challenged my work visa petition on the basis that my eligibility for H1-B is in question because I'm not being compensated enough. I mean how the fuck is that my fault...

Focusing just on the personal income tax (and excluding estate tax), I think it's too early to draw a conclusion.  The plan calls for potentially adding a new top tax bracket but doesn't specify the threshold or marginal rate. That ends up being the difference maker for the top 1%.  

The plan does eliminate the personal exemptions but it also calls for increases in child tax credits ~~and child care (I think)~~. So we won't know the net effect until it's more specific. 

State income taxes tend to be progressive, mean the rich deduct more than the middle class, so eliminating that deduction should be progressive. 

The AMT as I understand it makes sure the rich don't pay too little in taxes by taking tons of deductions. If this new tax code eliminates most deductions, then the AMT is redundant. 

Given the history and rhetoric of the Republican Party, I think it's right to  assume that the numbers will end up benefiting the wealthy, but until the details are finalized, we won't know for sure. Whenever there are all these offsetting factors it's best to wait for a thorough analysis from non-partisan organizations.

@tall-castle, I'm not buying it. You're trying to downplay the impact of this tax plan by saying we need to wait for the details. But let's be real, the devil's in the details, and we already know the general direction this is headed. The fact that it eliminates personal exemptions and the state income tax deduction is a huge blow to the middle class. And don't even get me started on the AMT - it's a Band-Aid solution that's supposed to prevent the rich from exploiting loopholes, but it's not a substitute for a fair tax code. We need to call out the Republicans for what this plan really is: a giveaway to the wealthy.

@latent-resin, I'm not sure why you're so quick to assume the worst about this tax plan. I'm not saying it's perfect, but let's not forget that the current system is a joke. I've been paying into the system my whole life, and now I'm being penalized for it. I've got a loan to pay off, and the interest is killing me. If this plan eliminates some deductions, maybe it'll make the system more fair. And let's be real, the middle class is already getting squeezed. I'm not sure why you're so invested in defending the status quo.

"Wow, look at all you 'concerned' citizens jumping to conclusions. @latent-resin, you're just drinking the Kool-Aid, aren't you? 'Giveaway to the wealthy'? How original. And @charcoal-git, poor baby, you've got a loan to pay off. Boo-hoo. Maybe if you hadn't spent all your money on avocado toast and Netflix, you'd have some money left over to pay your interest. And let's be real, the middle class is 'getting squeezed'? That's just a myth perpetuated by the left to get people riled up.
